summary:
Dense irregular connective tissue primarily occurs in tendons due to their need for strength under tension, and also in ligaments as they provide stability at joint attachments; both are richly supplied with tightly arranged collagen fibers essential for these functions. It can be noted that while present in other areas like parts of the dermis, tendon and ligament sites represent more specific examples fitting a detailed examination into anatomical structures requiring high mechanical resistance. To summarize:

Dense irregular connective tissue predominantly appears in tendons and ligaments because of their structural needs related to supporting body weight and stabilizing joints respectively. This type of tissue features tight arrangements of collagen fibers crucial for resisting stress effectively. ```plaintext
Key findings indicate that dense irregular connective tissue is chiefly located in tendons and ligaments owing to their functional requirements involving significant physical strain and stabilization. These tissues contain highly organized collagen fiber bundles necessary for enduring tensions experienced during movement or bodily load-bearing tasks.
```
